informal: Let $p(x)=a_{n} x^{n}+a_{n-1} x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_{1} x+a_{0}$ be an element of the polynomial ring $R[x]$. Prove that $p(x)$ is a zero divisor in $R[x]$ if and only if there is a nonzero $b \in R$ such that $b p(x)=0$.
lean:
theorem Dummit_Foote_exercise_7_2_2_corrected {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (p : Polynomial R) :
    (∃ q : Polynomial R, q ≠ 0 ∧ p * q = 0) ↔
      (∃ b : R, b ≠ 0 ∧ b • p = 0)
reasoning: The informal statement is McCoy's theorem: in R[x] (R commutative), p(x) is a zero divisor iff some nonzero constant b kills it. The Lean signature encodes "p is a zero divisor" as ∃ q ≠ 0, p*q = 0, which is the standard definition (a left/two-sided zero divisor in a commutative ring), and encodes "there is a nonzero b ∈ R with b·p(x) = 0" as ∃ b ≠ 0, b • p = 0, where scalar multiplication of a polynomial by an element of R is the natural action and is equivalent to multiplication by the constant polynomial C b. The CommRing R hypothesis matches the standard ambient setting (Dummit & Foote work in commutative rings here, and McCoy's theorem requires commutativity). The biconditional, the quantifier structure, and the types all match the informal claim.
